physical_ability_assumptions.ambulation: People can walk, stand, climb stairs, and navigate uneven terrain.
physical_ability_assumptions.manual_dexterity: People have two hands with usable fingers and sufficient fine motor control for tasks like typing, writing, grasping, and manipulating small objects.
physical_ability_assumptions.reach_and_stature: People have approximately average adult height, arm length, and physical strength, allowing interaction with doors, shelves, counters, and controls as designed.
physical_ability_assumptions.vision: People have functional eyesight sufficient for reading text, recognizing faces, and interpreting visual signage and cues.
physical_ability_assumptions.hearing: People can hear speech, alarms, announcements, and ambient auditory cues.
physical_ability_assumptions.speech: People can produce intelligible spoken language in ways that meet social and institutional expectations.
physical_ability_assumptions.physical_endurance: People can sit, stand, move, or remain alert for extended periods without undue fatigue or physical consequence.

cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.literacy: People can read and write at a functional adult level.
cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.numeracy: People can perform basic arithmetic, manage time, and handle money.
cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.attention_control: People can sustain focus, switch tasks, and filter distractions as required by typical environments.
cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.memory: People have sufficient short-term and working memory to follow instructions, remember sequences, and manage deadlines.
cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.processing_speed: People can comprehend information and respond within socially expected timeframes.
cognitive_and_perceptual_assumptions.executive_function: People can plan, prioritize, regulate impulses, and monitor their own behavior.

psychological_and_emotional_assumptions.emotional_regulation: People can manage emotions within socially tolerated bounds.
psychological_and_emotional_assumptions.stress_tolerance: People can function under noise, time pressure, uncertainty, and moderate stress.
psychological_and_emotional_assumptions.predictable_behavior: People’s mood and conduct are reasonably consistent across contexts.

social_and_communicative_assumptions.norm_interpretation: People can infer unwritten social rules and expectations.
social_and_communicative_assumptions.neurotypical_communication: People communicate and interpret communication using expected patterns of eye contact, tone, turn-taking, and facial expression.
social_and_communicative_assumptions.theory_of_mind: People can model others’ intentions, beliefs, and perspectives.
social_and_communicative_assumptions.self_advocacy: People can request help, articulate needs, and navigate bureaucratic or institutional systems.

temporal_and_logistical_assumptions.time_awareness: People can keep schedules, meet deadlines, and be punctual.
temporal_and_logistical_assumptions.independent_navigation: People can navigate physical and institutional spaces independently using maps, signage, and standard wayfinding systems.
temporal_and_logistical_assumptions.energy_predictability: People have relatively stable daily energy levels and functioning without sudden or severe depletion.

institutional_assumptions.independence_by_default: People require minimal assistance in work, education, healthcare, and daily life.
institutional_assumptions.consistency_over_accommodation: People can participate under uniform conditions without individualized adjustment.
institutional_assumptions.cost_neutral_access: Participation does not require significant additional personal resources, equipment, time, or support.

bodily_self_maintenance_assumptions.low_interoceptive_maintenance_requiremen: People can function safely without frequent attention to bodily signals (fatigue, pain, hunger, sensory overload, autonomic stress), and short-term neglect of self-care does not result in rapid or severe consequences.

stability_assumptions.functional_stability: People’s functional capacities, sensory thresholds, regulatory needs, and access requirements are generally stable and predictable over time rather than fluctuating, cyclical, or episodic.
